food_name: Spicy Beef Noodle Soup (Bún Bò Huế)
food_subgroup: Noodle soup with beef and pork

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bún Bò Huế; FOODON_00002473 (beef noodle soup food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR (soup, noodle, with meat) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Bún Bò Huế code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bún Bò Huế or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bún Bò Huế as typically served in a Vietnamese restaurant weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth, noodles, beef shank slices, pork knuckle pieces, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Bo Hue (Vietnamese Spicy Beef Noodle Soup) — nutrient composition estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for beef noodle soup and USDA FoodData Central component data for beef shank, rice noodles, and lemongrass broth
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Tables; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bún Bò Huế as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 40% broth, 30% round rice noodles, 20% beef shank, 10% pork knuckle and garnishes. FAO/INFOODS SEA data used for rice noodles and broth base; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for beef shank (NDB 13360) and pork knuckle (NDB 10219). Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and shrimp paste. Values represent a typical restaurant preparation per 100 g of total bowl weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bún Bò Huế. GI estimated at 50 based on the GI of rice vermicelli/rice noodles (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward for the mixed-dish effect: protein and fat from beef shank and pork knuckle slow gastric emptying and reduce the glycemic response. The composite GI of 50 is consistent with published mixed noodle soup GI values (range 45–55) in Atkinson et al. 2021. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 36.0) / 100 = 18.0, using 500 g serving with 7.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, shrimp paste, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl of Bún Bò Huế provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit broth consumption, and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 50 and a glycemic load of 18 per serving, Bún Bò Huế has a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a standard portion, prioritise the protein-rich beef and pork components, and avoid adding extra chilli oil or sweetened condiments that may increase caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef shank and pork knuckle
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2100 mg per bowl) and saturated fat (~4.5 g per bowl) from the meat components poses a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose leaner cuts where available, and avoid consuming the full volume of broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Bò Huế is a traditional Vietnamese spicy beef noodle soup originating from the imperial city of Huế in central Vietnam. It is distinct from the more internationally recognised Phở in several key ways: it uses thick, round rice noodles (bún) rather than flat rice noodles, and its broth is characterised by a bold, spicy, and aromatic flavour profile derived from lemongrass, shrimp paste (mắm ruốc), annatto oil for colour, chilli, fish sauce, and slow-simmered beef and pork bones. The soup typically contains sliced beef shank, pork knuckle, and sometimes pork blood cubes, served with fresh herbs, bean sprouts, lime wedges, and banana blossom. The broth is simmered for several hours to develop depth of flavour. Nutritionally, Bún Bò Huế is a moderate-energy dish (~340 kcal per 500 g bowl) with a reasonable protein content (~29 g per bowl) from the beef and pork, moderate carbohydrate from the rice noodles (~36 g per bowl), and notably high sodium (~2100 mg per bowl) due to the liberal use of fish sauce and shrimp paste. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low GI), as the protein and fat content of the meat components attenuate the glycemic response of the rice noodles. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium, making this dish unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not halal due to the presence of pork and pork-derived shrimp paste. In Singapore, Bún Bò Huế is available at Vietnamese restaurants and is increasingly popular as an alternative to Phở.
common_user_queries: How is Bún Bò Huế different from Pho?
What noodles are used in Bun Bo Hue?
How much sodium is in Bun Bo Hue?

What are the main ingredients in Bun Bo Hue?

ingredient_synonyms.bún: round rice noodles, rice vermicelli (thick), rice stick noodles
ingredient_synonyms.mắm ruốc: shrimp paste, fermented shrimp paste, Vietnamese shrimp sauce
ingredient_synonyms.giò heo: pork knuckle, pork hock, pork trotter
ingredient_synonyms.annatto oil: dầu điều, annatto seed oil, achiote oil

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bún Bò Huế as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and published mixed noodle soup values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (FAO/INFOODS SEA + USDA SR Legacy) as no direct composite dish entry exists in approved primary sources. The following micronutrients are reported as -1 (sentinel value for missing data) because they are not reliably reported in the component-level sources used for this estimation and cannot be defensibly aggregated without a direct dish analysis: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g as beef and pork at these quantities contribute negligible industrially-produced trans fat; any naturally occurring trans fat (conjugated linoleic acid) is below the threshold of practical significance per 100 g. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Bún Bò Huế has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. The dish contains pork and shrimp paste and is therefore not halal. Sodium values are particularly variable across restaurant preparations depending on broth concentration and seasoning; the value of 420 mg/100 g represents a typical mid-range estimate. Price data (SGD 20.90) is preserved from the field survey data source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
